a: An API is a way for two programs to talk to each other. One program asks for something, and the other program sends back an answer, following agreed-upon rules.
fs: An API is a contract that lets one program request data or actions from another without knowing how it works inside.
fj: Thinking an API is a whole website or app, instead of the messaging layer programs use to talk to each other.

a: An endpoint is a specific URL you send a request to in order to get one kind of thing. Each endpoint usually does one job, like listing users or fetching one user.
fs: An endpoint is a single addressable URL on an API that handles one resource or action.
fj: Assuming one API has just one URL, instead of many endpoints, each for a different piece of data.

a: A request is the message you send to an API to ask for something. It says what you want, which is often shown by a method like GET to read data or POST to send new data.
fs: A request is an outbound message naming a method, a URL, and optional headers or body for the server to act on.
fj: Confusing GET and POST: trying to send new data with GET when POST is the method for creating things.

a: A response is the answer the API sends back after your request. It includes a status code that says how it went, like 200 for success or 404 for not found, plus any data you asked for.
fs: A response is the server's reply carrying a status code and usually a body of data for the client to use.
fj: Ignoring the status code and assuming every response worked, even when it returned a 404 or 500 error.

a: JSON is a simple text format for sending data between programs. It uses keys and values inside curly braces, which makes it easy for both people and computers to read.
fs: JSON is a lightweight, text-based data format of key-value pairs and lists that most APIs use to send and receive data.
fj: Mixing up JSON text with a real object, or breaking it with a trailing comma or single quotes, which JSON disallows.
